questions.question: What are the three core criteria for Gaming Disorder according to WHO ICD-11?
options: Playing games daily, owning multiple consoles, and preferring games to other activities

Impaired control over gaming (frequency/duration), increasing priority of gaming over other activities/interests, and continuation or escalation despite negative consequences — resulting in significant impairment for 12+ months
Spending any money on games, playing competitive games, and gaming alone
Gaming Disorder is not a real clinical condition
questions.explanation: WHO ICD-11 Gaming Disorder requires: (1) Impaired control over gaming (onset, frequency, intensity, duration, termination); (2) Increasing priority given to gaming to the extent other activities are taken to insignificant degree; (3) Continuation/escalation despite negative consequences; (4) Pattern present for 12+ months (or shorter if severe); (5) Significant impairment in functioning. This affects ~2-3% of gamers — most gamers do NOT develop disorder.

questions.question: What is the key difference between healthy recreational gaming and problematic gaming?
options: Any gaming is unhealthy

Healthy gaming is freely chosen, time-bounded, balanced with other activities, and enhances functioning; Problematic gaming involves loss of control, displacement of essential activities (sleep/work/relationships), and functional impairment
The amount of time spent gaming determines health (any amount over 2 hours is problematic)
There is no difference
questions.explanation: Healthy recreational gaming is freely chosen, fits within reasonable time limits, doesn't displace essential activities, enhances overall functioning, and you can stop when needed. Problematic gaming involves difficulty stopping, displacement of sleep/work/relationships/self-care, use as primary coping mechanism, and continuation despite negative consequences. It's about control and function, not absolute time.

questions.question: According to the lesson, what is the evidence status for gaming as a mental health intervention?
options: Gaming is a proven clinical treatment for depression and anxiety

Evidence for gaming benefits is EMERGING/LIMITED (preliminary support for social connection, cognitive engagement, accessibility) — most research is correlational or small pilot studies; frame as adjunctive leisure, not proven treatment
All gaming research shows gaming is harmful
Gaming has the same evidence level as cognitive-behavioral therapy
questions.explanation: The evidence for gaming as mental health intervention is EMERGING/LIMITED. While mechanisms are theoretically sound (social connection, cognitive engagement, mastery), RCT-level evidence is sparse. Most gaming research is correlational or small pilot studies. Gaming Disorder has STRONG evidence as a clinical entity. Digital recreation can support wellbeing when balanced, but it's not a proven treatment — and risks exist when use becomes compulsive.

questions.question: What does the lesson recommend regarding sleep protection and digital recreation?
options: Gaming has no effect on sleep

Implement firm screen-off time 1 hour before bed (protects sleep onset and wind-down), no gaming in bed (bed = sleep only), and prioritize sleep over digital recreation — because sleep deprivation severely worsens mental health
You can game as late as you want without consequences
Only children need sleep boundaries
questions.explanation: Digital recreation is the #1 cause of voluntary sleep restriction, and sleep deprivation has severe mental health consequences (worsens depression, anxiety, emotion regulation). The lesson recommends: (1) All screens off 1 hour before bed; (2) No gaming in bed (conditioning: bed = sleep only); (3) Use blue light filters if needed, though behavioral boundaries are more effective. Sleep is non-negotiable for mental health.

questions.question: Why does the lesson recommend diversifying your leisure portfolio beyond digital recreation?
options: Digital activities provide no benefits

If digital recreation is your ONLY hobby, you're more vulnerable to problematic use because you have no alternative ways to meet underlying needs (social connection, mastery, stress relief) — diverse leisure reduces dependence on any single activity
You should never engage in digital recreation
Diversification has no effect on gaming patterns
questions.explanation: If digital recreation is your ONLY source of social connection, mastery, stress relief, or stress coping, you're at higher risk for problematic use because you have no alternatives. Diversifying leisure (adding offline physical recreation, in-person social activities, creative hobbies) means digital becomes ONE source for needs, not the ONLY source. This redundancy creates resilience and reduces vulnerability to Gaming Disorder.
